posts.name: Jake Rubash
posts.cooked: Birthday is coming up this Saturday and this is the wine we plan to drink. What’s the word on how long to have this open/decanted before consumption?

posts.name: Glenn L e v i n e
posts.cooked: Reading w/ interest as I have 2 bottles.
posts.display_username: Glenn L e v i n e

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked: There is BIG bottle variation with the 1991. Essential to try in advance to ensure you got one of the good ones.

posts.name: Ry@n S m 1t h
posts.cooked:
For the 1991 MB, I would stand the bottle upright for a few days to let the sediment collect at the bottom. I decanted mine through cheesecloth to catch the remainder.

As far as decant times, it was showing well after around 45 minutes in decanter. If it’s anything like the bottle I had, you are in for a treat. One of the best Monte Bellos ever!

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
Jake–I would stand it upright at least a day or two before opening; how much sediment is in your bottle?

As for decanting, there is so much variation now in something like a 1991 Ridge Monte Bello that it’s hard to predict. If someone bought the bottle on release and stored it perfectly, it will still be a bit on the young side and could use a few hours in the decanter. If it hasn’t been stored perfectly since release, you won’t need that much decanter time, and you can let the wine open up in the glass after a relatively short time in the decanter.

Bruce

posts.name: David Gerstenfeld
posts.cooked: I just drank a bottle last night. After 1 hour in the decanter it was spectacular. It was the 10th bottle of a case I bought years ago and every single bottle has been great. I’ve never seen any bottle variation with this wine over the last 20 years of drinking it.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked: Based on a few recent bottles, I would think an hour would be plenty. Great wine.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Personally, I wouldn’t decant, except perhaps to pour it off its sediment. For wines of this age, I like to just pour it in the glass and watch it evolve.

A few years ago, Ridge poured the 90/91/92 trio at an event up on the hill. I hung around for quite a while and sampled at least 3 different bottles of each. As for variation, it’s all about the bottle. I’ve had bottles that seemed young, and others that seemed already tired and on the tail end of their plateau. Even the best, “youngest” seeming bottles I’ve experienced in recent years show the 91 to be well into it’s plateau, maybe even past the middle. For me, it’s the 92 that seems to be a longer lived wine at this point.

posts.name: Chuck Miller
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:
ybarselah:
DavidZ:

There is BIG bottle variation with the 1991.

utter nonsense.

Then you haven’t had enough bottles of it. I’ve had 4 and 2 were amazing and 2 - from the same source - were weirdly muted. Discussion with others revels that many have similar experiences. People don’t talk about it publicly on the boards because they don’t want to deal with people like you calling them liars.

The bad bottles are muted and oaky and very un MB like. FWIW, I’ve had pretty much every vintage of MB going back to 1985.

So you’ve had 2 bottles of a limited sampling of 4 btls (both of which you described as muted, which I would guess means they might have had low-level TCA) and you categorically state that there is ‘BIG bottle variation’?? In practically every bottle of 24 year old wine (in my limited experience drinking thousands of such bottles), there will be some variation.

Now if you had phrased it ‘In my limited experience, there might be some bottle variation’, fine. Otherwise, you are just a troll. Feel free to review the many CT reviews and show me where there is ‘BIG bottle variation’. Of course, people don’t talk about it privately, so you must be right.

posts.name: Mike Silverman
posts.cooked: I bought two cases on release. I have had 22 bottles thus far. All have ben perfect. I would echo those who say stand the bottle for a day (a fair amount of sediment). i would splash decant and let it sit for half an hour. One of the joys of this wine is to follow the changes in the glass over a few hours. Enjoy. Happy Birthday.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Gone through seven bottles, all pristine.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Chuck Miller:

So you’ve had 2 bottles of a limited sampling of 4 btls (both of which you described as muted, which I would guess means they might have had low-level TCA) and you categorically state that there is ‘BIG bottle variation’?? In practically every bottle of 24 year old wine (in my limited experience drinking thousands of such bottles), there will be some variation.

Now if you had phrased it ‘In my limited experience, there might be some bottle variation’, fine. Otherwise, you are just a troll. Feel free to review the many CT reviews and show me where there is ‘BIG bottle variation’. Of course, people don’t talk about it privately, so you must be right.

4 bottles is “limited experience”? On what planet? Serious, do Parker, Galloni et al refrain from posting notes on bottles until theyve had a bakers dozen?

It came up at two tastings with different groups and both groups had folks who said there was big bottle variation and they’d run into the duds. The duds have a distinct character so you’ll know it when you find one. It’s also obvious if you look at CT - many more bad notes with the 1991 compared to surrounding years. Many rapturous notes as well, but that’s the point. I don’t know if it’s bottle variation originating at Ridge or if the bottles have simply changed hands so many times that a bunch are fried, but the net result - that the performance of clean looking bottles of 1991 from reliable sellers is much more uncertain than the 1992, or the 1990, etc - is widely known.

Setting all this aside, the 1992 is a better wine today and if the 1991 was ever a “reference point” it isn’t anymore. Blind tasting will illustrate that quite clearly.

It’s also obvious if you look at CT - many more bad notes with the 1991 compared to surrounding years. Many rapturous notes as well, but that’s the point.

posts.name: Walt Hoehler
posts.cooked:
Fellas, the 1991 ranks among the single greatest red wines ever to be produced in the state of California and any thread that references this standout grabs my interest. The '92 and '96 are also top notch.

To the OP, a couple days upright and 30-90 mins uncorked is all you should need at this point but every bottle has its own history by the time they are twenty years old so YMMV.

To everyone else, please use the ignore function. It works great.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: The 91 is so good. The 90 and 92 aren’t far behind. These wines are the main reason MB is my most obvious no brainier CA purchase every year.

posts.name: Barry P
posts.cooked: I’ve probably had ten bottles in my lifetime. One of which was a mag. Never a bad bottle. All singing. All in perfect or nearly so. A stunning wine. It is a reference point in Cal cab and, for me, sits along with the likes of 68 and 70 Mayacamas, 74 Heitz Marthas, 78 DC Lake, 91 Dominus, 92 Maya, 94 Harlan (now that’s bottle variation), 95 Araujo and 97 Bryant.

counselor, i believe the “law of holes” applies aptly.

en.wikipedia.org

Law of holes

The law of holes, or the first law of holes, is an adage which states: "if you find yourself in a hole, stop digging." It is used as a metaphor, warning that when in an untenable position, it is best to stop making the situation worse. When it is said, "if you find yourself in a hole, stop digging", it is because digging a hole makes it deeper and therefore harder to get out of. More generally, the adage advises how one should solve problems of their own making. The second law of holes is com...

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Interesting wine. I last had this about a year ago, and discussed it with Eric Baugher at Ridge. Apparently it’s one of the lowest tannin vintages of Monte Bello ever made. But it is still very vibrant and youthful for all that. Its unusually luscious style makes it rather singular.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:
David Gerstenfeld:

I just drank a bottle last night. After 1 hour in the decanter it was spectacular. It was the 10th bottle of a case I bought years ago and every single bottle has been great. I’ve never seen any bottle variation with this wine over the last 20 years of drinking it.

My guess is that another name for bottle variation in this case is that the person posting had heat damaged wines. You bought your wines years ago (as you said) and took care of them.

I believe this is often true of wines purchased years after release without knowledge of how they were stored. Which is why I tend to ignore assessments of these types of bottles. It would be useful for the reader if those who post about their experiences with older wines to indicate how/when/where they were purchased and, if known, stored. YMMV.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Let it stand in the cellar for a week before. Remove cork a couple of hours before serving, then decant when you are ready to go. I really love this wine, and along with the 1996 are my two favorite wines from this producer.

Last time I had it, it was absolutely stunning, and perfectly mature.
